coredata.dc:title: Turmeric carbon quantum dots enhanced chitosan nanocomposite films based on photodynamic inactivation technology for antibacterial food packaging
coredata.dc:creator: Wen, Fangzhou
Li, Peiyuan
Yan, Hongjun
Su, Wei
coredata.dc:description: The increased demand for food quality and safety has led the food industry to pay urgent attention to new packaging materials with antimicrobial activity. In this study, we combined photodynamic inactivation of bactericidal technology in food packaging materials by incorporating fluorescent carbon quantum dots (CDs) prepared from the natural plant turmeric into a chitosan matrix to prepare a series of active composite food packaging films (CDs-CS). The chitosan film containing CDs had better mechanical properties, UV protection and hydrophobicity. Under irradiation with a 405 nm light source, the composite film was able to produce abundant reactive oxygen species, and the CDs-CS2 film exhibited reductions of approximately 3.19 and 2.05 Log10 CFU/mL for Staphylococcus aureus and Escherichia coli respectively within 40 min. In cold pork storage applications, CDs-CS2 films showed inhibition of the growth of colonization in pork and retarded the spoilage of pork within 10 days. This work will provide new insights to explore safe and efficient antimicrobial food packaging.

head.ce:title: Turmeric carbon quantum dots enhanced chitosan nanocomposite films based on photodynamic inactivation technology for antibacterial food packaging

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The increased demand for food quality and safety has led the food industry to pay urgent attention to new packaging materials with antimicrobial activity. In this study, we combined photodynamic inactivation of bactericidal technology in food packaging materials by incorporating fluorescent carbon quantum dots (CDs) prepared from the natural plant turmeric into a chitosan matrix to prepare a series of active composite food packaging films (CDs-CS). The chitosan film containing CDs had better mechanical properties, UV protection and hydrophobicity. Under irradiation with a 405 nm light source, the composite film was able to produce abundant reactive oxygen species, and the CDs-CS2 film exhibited reductions of approximately 3.19 and 2.05 Log10 CFU/mL for Staphylococcus aureus and Escherichia coli respectively within 40 min. In cold pork storage applications, CDs-CS2 films showed inhibition of the growth of colonization in pork and retarded the spoilage of pork within 10 days. This work will provide new insights to explore safe and efficient antimicrobial food packaging.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: White pollution has exacerbated the environmental crisis and traditional non-degradable petroleum-based plastic packaging materials are gradually being replaced by reactive packaging films based on natural polymeric materials (Larson & Klibanov, 2013; Zhong et al., 2020). The latter can improve the internal environment of the package and ensure the extension of shelf life in maintaining the original food quality (Malhotra et al., 2015). The natural polymers have been widely employed as matrices to prepare active packaging films, including starch (Praseptiangga et al., 2021), dextrin (Islamipour et al., 2022) cellulose (Roy & Rhim, 2020), polysaccharides (Nešić et al., 2019), proteins (V & Badwaik, 2022) and so on. Composite films composed of modified corn starch and gelatin showed excellent mechanical properties and could extend the shelf life of cucumber (Kumar et al., 2021). However, the lack of antimicrobial capacity of these films makes microorganisms in food products accelerate spoilage and deterioration, which is a major problem for food quality and shelf life.

As a type of active packaging material, antibacterial packaging film is used to control the growth and reproduction of microorganisms by releasing antibacterial additives from the carrier matrix, protecting the nutritional flavors of the food while achieving long-lasting and effective preservation (Janani et al., 2020; Valencia et al., 2019). Chitosan, the deacetylated product of chitin, is the most commonly used antibacterial film material today due to its anti-inflammatory and antibacterial effects (Cheung et al., 2015; Patrulea et al., 2015). The positively charged amine groups in chitosan can interact electrostatically with the negative charges of microbial cell walls, causing leakage of components within the cell membrane and leading to apoptosis (Abd El-Hack et al., 2020). However, the narrow antibacterial spectrum of chitosan makes it necessary to combine other active agents or therapies for the design of chitosan-based active packaging films that can effectively kill microorganism, protect the nutritional flavor of food, and achieve long-lasting preservation.

Photodynamic inactivation technology (PDT) is a reliable and innovative antimicrobial treatment method with mild conditions and negligible damage to normal tissue cells (Li et al., 2020; Maisch et al., 2005; Mao et al., 2019). It kills bacteria by absorbing a light source through a photosensitizer (PS) and interacting with oxygen to produce reactive oxygen species, with bacteria unlikely to develop resistance (Zhang, Li, et al., 2022, p.; Zhang, Yan, et al., 2022). The Ce6 loaded CS covalently functionalized MoS2 nanocomposite (M-CS-Ce6) prepared by Cao et al. can achieve enhanced photodynamic antibacterial action against Gram-positive bacteria through hyperpolarization of bacterial films and enhancement of ROS levels (Cao et al., 2022). Li's study prepared Ag-doped TiO2-chitosan composites by inverse emulsion cross-linking reaction and demonstrated that the synergistic sustained release of nano-TiO2 and silver ions enhanced the inhibition of Pseudomonas aeruginosa and Staphylococcus aureus under visible light (Li et al., 2018). However, metal ions can accumulate in the human body and may cause serious disease. These inorganic ions or synthetic photosensitizers (PSs) not only brings production costs, but also increases the safety crisis of direct contact with food, making them unsuitable for use in food packaging.

Carbon quantum dots (CDs), the latest addition to the family of carbon-based nanomaterials, are considered to be a promising option for PSs due to their excellent optical properties, good biocompatibility and low toxicity, which are widely used in antibacterial, anticancer and drug delivery applications. In recent research，carboxymethylcellulose films loaded with chitosan carbon quantum dots showed excellent antibacterial properties and could inhibit bacterial growth on lemon (Riahi et al., 2022); Chitosan composite films with carbon quantum dots from raw materials such as citric acid or discarded fish scales, exhibited enhanced UV shielding properties and antibacterial action (Fu et al., 2022; Wang et al., 2022). More recently, CDs from natural extracts of herbs and plants are considered to be ideal sources of PSs, which are more worthy of attention because of their abundant sources and safety. However, to the best of our knowledge, so far it has not reported the natural CDs loaded antimicrobial films with PDT effect for food preservation.

Turmeric, an important medicinal and dietary perennial herb dried rhizome, is widely used throughout medicine, food and health care for its anti-inflammatory, antibacterial, anti-cancer, and hypoglycemic and other biological activities (Guest, 2021; Kocaadam & Şanlier, 2017). The main active ingredient of turmeric, curcumin (Cur), has been recognized as a natural food additive by WHO and FDA, which is also a natural, cheap and easily available PS (Zheng et al., 2020; Zorofchian Moghadamtousi et al., 2014). However, the poor water solubility and stability of Cur hinder its application as PS in food industry (Khoshkalampour et al., 2023; Riahi et al., 2022). According to our previous study, the CDs using turmeric as a precursor can not only overcome the water solubility and stability problems of curcumin and present low toxicity as well as good biocompatibility, but also possessed efficient killing effect against a wide range of bacteria with blue light irradiation (Yan et al., 2022), indicating that turmeric CDs showed great potential as PSs in antibacterial food packaging film. In view of these advantages, we envisaged that the antimicrobial spectrum of chitosan films could be expanded by introducing turmeric CDs to construct a green, active food packaging film with a light-driven PDT strategy that shows great potential for food preservation.

In this paper, by combining photodynamic technology, a novel CDs loaded chitosan-based active packaging material with enhanced antimicrobial activity was prepared, and its properties as food packaging were investigated. The effects of incorporation of turmeric CDs with different composition ratios on the physicochemical properties such as microstructure, thermal stability, mechanical and barrier properties of CS films were comprehensively investigated. Furthermore, the antimicrobial activity was tested by E. coli and S. aureus and in the presence or absence of light irradiation. Finally, the practical application effect of the composite film was examined by packaging fresh pork. It is expected to provide new ideas and technical support for the food packaging industry to develop safe and environmentally friendly packaging technology (Fig. 1 ).

Low molecular weight chitosan with molecular weight of 50–200 kDa and deacetylation degree ≥95 % was provided by Aladdin Reagent (Shanghai) Co. Turmeric was purchased from a local pharmacy. Acetic acid (CH3COOH), ethanol (CH3COOH) were purchased from Aladdin Reagent (Shanghai) Co. Nutrient agar and nutrient broth were purchased from Beijing Luqiao Technology Co. Escherichia coli and Staphylococcus aureus were purchased from Shanghai Lu Wei Technology Co. All reagents in the experiments were of analytical grade and high purity.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of films of CS and CDs-CS
ce:section.ce:para: CDs were prepared by hydrothermal method using turmeric as raw material, according to our previous method (Yan et al., 2022). Briefly, 2 g of turmeric powder was mixed with 30 mL of ethanol, then sonicated and centrifuged and filtered through a microporous membrane to obtain the turmeric extract. 20 mL of turmeric extract was transferred to an autoclave and heated in an oven at 180 °C for 12 h. The reaction solution was successively collected by centrifugation and 0.22 μm microporous membrane and dried in an oven at 60 °C to obtain CQDs.

1 g of CS was added to 100 mL of acetic acid (1 %) and stirred at 45 °C for 4 h to configure 1 % w/t CS solution. 1 mL of CDs solution (0.5, 1 and 2 mg/mL) were added to 20 mL of chitosan solution, respectively, which was mixed in a ratio of 1:20, and stirred continuously for 12 h to form the film-forming solution. The film-forming solution was centrifuged at 3000 r/min for 10 min to eliminate air bubbles and large particles of precipitation, then poured evenly into plastic molds (9 mm × 9 mm) and dried at 35–40 °C for 24–48 h to obtain a series of composite films. The films with pure chitosan, 0.25 wt%, 0.5 wt% and 1 wt% carbon quantum dot concentrations (based on chitosan weight) were defined as CS, CDs-CS1, CDs-CS2, CDs-CS3, respectively. The films were placed at 25 °C and 50 % humidity for 1–2 days, and then stored in a desiccator away from light for use.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) and spectral properties
ce:section.ce:para: The surface morphology of the film samples was observed by SEM. The film samples (3 cm × 1 cm) were scanned on an ultraviolet-visible (UV–Vis) spectrophotometer, and the transmittance of the films was measured using an empty test tube as a reference. The infrared spectra of the film samples were measured using an infrared spectrometer.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Chroma value, thickness, and mechanical properties
ce:section.ce:para: Film color was measured using a colorimeter, using white card stock as a standard (Hu et al., 2020). The thickness of the film samples was obtained by using a digital spiral micrometer with 5 measurements each. Film samples (2.5 cm × 0.6 cm) were fixed in a universal mechanical testing machine (AGS-X, Shimadzu) and stretched at a deformation rate of 100 mm/s to determine the tensile strength (TS) and elongation at break (EAB) of the films (Jridi et al., 2014).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Moisture content (MC), water solubility (WS), water vapor permeability (WVP), oxygen transmission rate (ORT) and water contact angle (WCA)
ce:section.ce:para: Film sample (3 cm × 3 cm, M1) was first equilibrated at 20 °C and 50 % RH for 48 h and then dried at 105 °C to a constant weight (M2). Then stirred in 100 mL of distilled water at room temperature for 24 h. The insoluble film sample was taken out and further dried to a constant weight (M3) at 105 °C (Su et al., 2021).

Film sample (4 cm × 4 cm) was sealed on the opening of 50 mL centrifuge tube containing 20 g of completely dried silica gel. Then the centrifuge tube was placed in a desiccator containing distilled water at 20 °C and 50 % RH and weighed every 12 h for 3 days. The WVP of film was calculated as follows:
WVP =
W × x
t × A × ΔP
where WVP was water vapor permeability (g m−1 s−1 Pa−1), W was the weight gain of centrifuge tube (g), x was film thickness (m), t was time (s), A was the permeation area of film sample (m2), and ΔP was saturated vapor pressure at 20 °C.

The ORT was determined using an oxygen permeability tester (MOCON 2/21, MOCON, USA). The test was carried out in continuous mode at 23 °C and 52 % relative humidity. The WCA was measured using a contact angle meter (JC2000D, Shanghai Zhongchen Digital Technology Equipment) to assess the surface wettability of the film.

The thermal properties of the film samples were determined by differential scanning calorimetry. 4 mg of film samples were heated from 25 to 600 °C at a constant rate of 10 °C/min. All measurements were carried out under nitrogen atmosphere.

1,3-Diphenylbenzofuran (DPBF) was selected as the single-linear oxygen trapping agent, and a master batch was prepared with dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO) at a concentration of 5.0 × 10−5 mol/L, and the whole operation was carried out under protection from light. The film sample was attached to the inner wall of a (3 cm × 1 cm) cuvette, and 2 mL of deionized water and 36 μL of DPBF master batch were added to the cuvette to determine the absorption spectra of the solution after irradiation with 405 nm light source, and no film was added to the control group.

The colony counting method was used to assess the antibacterial activity of the nanocomposite membrane against foodborne pathogenic Gram-negative (E. coli) and Gram-positive (S. aureus) bacteria. The bacterial broth was cultured and diluted to about OD600 = 0.5. First, the films (1 cm × 1 cm) were laid flat in sterile well plates and 100 μL of bacterial suspension (2 × 107 CFU/mL) was evenly distributed on the film. After conducting different time of 405 nm light or dark treatment, the films were diluted to 2 mL with sterile saline and blown to mix the bacterial solution, and 100 μL of the diluted solution was spread on the agar plate and incubated in the incubator at 37 °C for 24 h. The antibacterial performance of the films was evaluated by colony counting method.

Fresh pork was cut into small pieces of 2 cm × 2 cm × 1 cm and weighed. Pork samples were randomly divided into four treatment groups: no packaging film group, normal cling film treatment group, CS film treatment group, and CDs-CS2 film treatment group. The bacterial colony counts of pork in each group before packaging were recorded as Day0. After wrapping the meat with different films, the meat was placed in petri dishes with a diameter of 9 cm and irradiated with a 405 nm light source for 40 min. All meat samples were stored in sealed plastic boxes at 4 °C for 10 days and taken out on days 1, 3, 5, 7 and 10 to be weighed and tested for colony count: the meat was transferred to sterile homogenization bags. After proper coating, homogenization, and dilution, 100 μL of suspension was applied on agar plates and incubated for a period to calculate the colony count.

Migration of packaging films was tested using a 3 % acetic acid (w/v) aqueous solution as a food simulant. Briefly, multiple film samples (1 × 1 cm) were immersed in 2 mL of the simulant. After immersion for various times, the film samples were removed and the UV−vis spectra of the residual simulant were analyzed (Li et al., 2021).

Sensory evaluation of pork was performed by 10 trained experts (5 men and 5 women, aged 20–40 years) from the population. A 5-point descriptive scale (5 = excellent, 4 = good, 3 = acceptable, 2 = unacceptable, 1 = very poor) was used to assess the color, odor, and overall acceptability of the meat samples (Bi et al., 2021).

All experiments were repeated at least three times, and all data are expressed as mean ± standard deviation (SD). The results were statistically analyzed by one-way analysis of variance (ANOVA). Statistical significance is denoted as follows: *p < 0.05.

The dispersion of nanoparticles in the surfaces of pure CS and CDs-CS composite films can be visually evaluated by SEM plots. As shown in the Fig. 2 , the films presented a dense and compact, uniform, and smooth surface without any voids or cracks. As the concentration increased, the films became inhomogeneous and appeared aggregated with tiny particles, but without significant agglomeration. Similar results were also observed in the earlier report by K et al. (2019). With the increase in the addition of mango leaf extract from 1 % to 5 %, the chitosan films showed a slight increase in surface roughness but still maintained a smooth structure. There was no significant difference between the film samples and addition of CDs had a negligible effect on the surface morphology of the chitosan film. The results indicated that CDs could be uniformly distributed in the CS matrix, and the film components were well distributed and highly compatible.

The spectra of CDs powder, CS and CDs-CS composite films are shown in Fig. 3 . The broad peaks formed around 3430 cm−1 were the absorption peaks of hydroxyl and amino groups of stretching vibration, 2925 cm−1 was the absorption peak of CH stretching vibration on the chitosan ring; the peak at 1648 cm−1 was attributed to the bending vibration of carbonyl and imide groups, 1407 cm−1 and the peaks at 1027 cm−1 were attributed to the symmetric CO stretching vibration of CS and the stretching vibration of CC stretching group, respectively (Halász & Csóka, 2018; Lawrie et al., 2007). The absorption peak of CDs at 2941 cm−1 was attributed to the stretching vibration of CH. The peaks near 1604 cm−1 and 1519 cm−1 were attributed to CO and CC stretching vibrations, respectively, and the adsorption peak at 1276 cm−1 was due to C-O-C bending vibrations.

The spectra of the CDs-CS composite films were similar to the peak pattern of CS, with no significant wavelength shift, probably due to the lower content of loaded CDs, which was also found in other similar studies (Agarwal et al., 2021; Yong et al., 2019). With increasing CDs, there is a clear widening trend in the intensity of the spectral bands of the films, the wideband broadening between 3500– 3000 cm−1 may be influenced by the abundant hydrogen bonds in the carbon quantum dots and overlap with the NH bonds on the amino groups. The results indicated that the addition of CDs did not change the overall structure of chitosan films, and there were mostly non-covalent and hydrogen bonding interactions between them.

The optical properties of food packaging films determine the appearance of the food inside the package and directly affect consumer acceptance. The color values (L*, a*, b*) and the total color difference value ∆E of CDs-CS laminated films are shown in the Table 1 . The L* values of the films varied from 99.9 to 81.0 with reduced brightness, but all films were basically transparent. The negative values of a*, b* indicated the absence of red and yellow color in the pure CS composite films. With the increase of CDs, the a* value tended to increase and the b* value increased significantly, indicating that the yellow color of the composite films tended to be more pronounced, which showed the yellow appearance typical of carbon quantum dots. The difference was significant (p < 0.05) for CDs-CS1, CDs-CS2 and CDs-CS3. Therefore, the CDs content influenced the total color difference (ΔE) of the CDs-CS composite films, which showed yellow color and good brightness and were still visually acceptable.

UV light in the near-UV region (in the range of 200 to 380 nm), one of the common oxidation initiators, can cause food spoilage (Thi Nguyen et al., 2022). Therefore, it is important to avoid lipid oxidation caused by UV light. Fig. 4A shows the transmittance values of the composite films in the near-UV and visible light ranges (200 to 800 nm) and a visual comparison of the UV protection of the films by the transmittance at 660 nm and 280 nm (Table 2 ). All films had a high transmittance of 84.09 % in the visible region at 660 nm. In the UV region of 280 nm, with the increase of CDs content, the light transmission of the laminated film decreased, indicating the CDs-CS3 film had a better UV-blocking ability. There are two possible reasons for this, one is that combined with the SEM image, we can see that due to the aggregation of nano carbon dots, tiny particles filled the gaps of the film matrix, which will affect the light transmission and lead to the reduction of light transmission. Secondly, the carbon quantum dots darken the yellow color of the film and the polyphenols in turmeric have a strong UV absorbing capacity, which reduced the light transmission of the film. Similarly, the addition of anthocyanins and alizarin to the chitosan film also resulted in enhanced UV resistance (Ezati & Rhim, 2020; Ma et al., 2018; Sun et al., 2017).

20 mL of different film-forming solutions were cast uniformly in a 9 cm × 9 cm mold. After drying into films, the thickness was measured as shown in the Table 2. Composite films with 0.5 % CDs content were not significantly different compared to CS films. The thickness of the composite films increased with the addition of CDs, with a maximum film thickness of 0.038 mm. As shown in Fig. 4B, the composite films can be repeatedly spread, bent and folded without breaking, which are suitable for the packaging of food with different shapes.

The tensile stress (TS) and elongation at break (EAB) were used as indicators to study the mechanical strength and ductility properties of the prepared composite films as food packaging films. As shown in Fig. 4C, the TS and EAB of the CS films were 30.41 ± 1.38 MPa and 66.11 ± 0.47 %, respectively, and the TS values of the lower concentrations of CDs-CS1 composite films did not change significantly after the addition of CDs, similar to those of plastic films such as HDPE (22– 31 MPa), which have been widely used in food packaging. The TS values of CDs-CS2 composite films increased, approaching polypropylene (31– 38 MPa) packaging materials. The high concentration of CDs-CS3 composite film showed a significant decrease in the TS value, due to the dense concentration of CDs weakening the interaction between CS molecules, resulting in a change in the internal structure of the matrix. The same change in the EAB value was also observed. These values are significantly better than most biopolymer films and films prepared in similar reports, such as sodium alginate (12.8 MPa, 11 %), poly (3.4 MPa, 14 %), gelatin (27.2 MPa, 14.5 %), polystyrene (30 MPa, 4 %), and CC/BB film (29.8 MPa, 38.65 %)(F. Li et al., 2021), CH-EM2.0 (18.05 MPa, 11.68 %) (Zhao et al., 2022), LEC-9 (30.98 MPa, 24.32 %) (Agarwal et al., 2021), CS-RB5 (28.48 MPa, 33.58 %) (Su et al., 2021). It can be seen that CDs-CS2 composite films have superior tensile strength and flexibility.

In terms of the water content of the films, the water content of the composite films decreased when CDs were added, and the water content of CDs-CS3 decreased the most significantly by 8.08 %. The water solubility of the membrane reflects the hydrophilic property of the membrane, which is affected by water diffusion, ionization of intermolecular hydrogen bonds, amino or carboxyl groups, and the internal loose structure of the membrane (Tagrida et al., 2023). Compared with CS films, the water solubility of composite films significantly reduced (P < 0.05), and the smallest water solubility of films was 7.72 %. It may be because carbon quantum dots contain a large number of active phenolic hydroxyl groups, which can form hydrogen bonds with the amino groups in the chitosan matrix, making the internal structure more stable and inhibiting the leaching of water. The water contact angle measurements basically reflect the strength of the hydrophilicity of the membrane and allow assessing the durability of the package. Films with water contact angle >90° are hydrophobic, and the WCA values of CS and composite films showed that they were both hydrophobic materials. The contact angle of the films was slightly reduced after loading with carbon quantum dot particles, but the difference was not significant, probably due to the low incorporation of carbon quantum dots.

As a food packaging material, it is important to reduce water loss and prevent oxidation of food. The water vapor transmission rate and oxygen transmission rate of the film reflects its water and oxygen barrier effect (Yun et al., 2021). It is clear that with the increase of CDs, the WVP value decreased. Similarly, the composite film exhibited excellent oxygen barrier properties compared to the CS film, and ORT decreased with increasing carbon point content. These changes can be explained by the fact that the addition of CDs makes the structure of the film more compact and orderly, and increases the film thickness, as a result, it makes the water vapor more difficult to go through the pores of the film thus improving the gas barrier performance of the film (Indumathi et al., 2019; Tamimi et al., 2021). The film can be selected to respond to the oxygen requirements of different food packaging (Fig. 5 ).

The thermal stability of the composite films with different contents was evaluated by TGA, and the thermal weight loss curves are shown in Fig. 6A. The thermal weight loss of chitosan film was mainly divided into three stages: the first mass loss occurred at the initial stage of temperature rise from 50 to 110 °C, with a mass loss rate of about 17.2 %, which was mainly attributed to the volatilization of bound water, and the curve gradually leveled off when the temperature reached 100 °C. The second mass loss appeared at 280– 350 °C, which was the main weight loss stage with about 35 % mass loss. It is mainly due to the thermal-oxidative decomposition of chitosan polymer chains, glycosidic bond breakage and dehydration of adjacent hydroxyl groups (Lee et al., 2023). Compared with chitosan films, the thermal stability of chitosan composite films with CDs was enhanced, especially CDs-CS3. The third mass loss occurred in the interval of 400–600 °C, and the film continued to degrade slowly with the increase of temperature. By 600 °C, the residual rate of the composite film increased from 35 % to 37.3 % while the weight loss rate decreased with the increase of CDs content in the film. Reasons for this are as reported (Jha, 2020; Khoshkalampour et al., 2023), CDs can catch water molecules and maintain thermal stability through hydrogen bonds formed with the matrix. The results showed that the CDs were well incorporated into the films and its incorporation did not affect the original thermal stability of the CS films.

Singlet oxygen is considered as a key factor in photodynamic effects, which can induce apoptosis, cell necrosis and cell autophagy. The quantum yields of singlet oxygen in CS and CDs-CS2 films were tested using 1,3-diphenylisobenzofuran (DPBF) as singlet oxygen trapping agent (Wen et al., 2022). As shown in the Fig. 6B–D, under the irradiation of 405 nm light source, CDs-CS2 decreased the absorbance of DPBF at a relatively fast rate, which indicated that CDs-CS2 had a relatively good ability to produce singlet oxygen. Even if CDs were loaded in chitosan matrix, they can be effectively activated by UV irradiation to produce singlet oxygen to exert antibacterial effect.

Fig. 7 shows the differences in the antibacterial performance of the composite films against Gram-negative bacteria Escherichia coli and Gram-positive bacteria Staphylococcus aureus. It can be seen that the pure chitosan film without CDs showed some antibacterial ability compared with the blank control without 405 nm irradiation, proving that chitosan was equipped with certain antibacterial properties, but the bacterial survival rate was still high in the chitosan film treated group.

With the addition of CDs, the inhibitory effect of the composite films on bacteria was significantly increased, and the bacterial survival rate decreased substantially with the increase of light time. CDs-CS2 with 0.5 % CDs content showed 92.75 % antibacterial rate against S. aureus and 68.5 % antibacterial rate against E. coli after 20 min of light irradiation. Extending the light exposure time to 40 min, CDs-CS2 showed the strongest antibacterial ability, with S. aureus and E. coli exhibiting a reduction of about 3.19 and 2.05 Log10 CFU/mL, respectively. In a more recent study, Yun et al. (2021) also discovered that the inhibitory activity of CS-RPE films increased with the amount of admixed extract, with CS- RPE III films showing at least 1.38 times the inhibitory activity of CS films. CDs-CS2 showed at least 1.48 times higher inhibitory activity than CS films under 40 min of light. The antibacterial effect of the composite film is higher against Gram-positive bacteria than Gram-negative bacteria, possibly due to differences in the structure of the Gram-negative bacterial cell membrane, and Gram-negative bacteria are more sensitive to the composite film, which is also seen in a similar study (Zhao et al., 2022). It was observed that the antibacterial effect of CDs-CS2 was better than that of CDs-CS3 in the graph of the inhibition effect of the composite film under prolonged light exposure, probably because the exorbitant content of carbon nanodots will lead to the particles agglomeration, as a result, the density of surface-active dots decreased and the antibacterial effect was slightly reduced. Because of the best antibacterial properties among the three carbon-dotted chitosan membranes as well as the good physical properties and best tensile resistance, CDs-CS2 were chosen for the subsequent experiments.

Fresh pork was selected to evaluate the antimicrobial effect of CS film and CDs-CS2 film. Fig. 8B shows the changes in total bacterial counts of pork in different treatment groups during 7 d of storage. Among them, we selected pork without wrapping film as control 1 and pork wrapped in normal food cling film as control 2. A non-significantly different initial flora of approximately 4 Log10 CFU/ml was present on the pork samples before packaging and light treatment. On the first day of storage, only a few bacteria survived in the CDs-CS2 film-packed pork samples after light treatment. As can be visualized by the digital pictures, the total bacterial colony count increased significantly with time, especially in the control group, the total bacterial count already exceeded 106 CFU/mL after 3 days, and the pork was close to spoilage which is not of acceptable quality for marketing (Su et al., 2021). As previously reported, chitosan films also slow down the growth of bacteria somewhat in application (Pabast et al., 2018; C. Wang et al., 2020). CDs-CS2 had higher bacterial inhibition rates than pure chitosan films and normal cling films. Until the 7th day of storage, the total number of bacteria in the CDs-CS2 group (104.5 CFU/mL) was still below the maximum acceptable limit (107). The total colony count of the control group increased at a fast rate during storage, with an increase of 3.72 and 3.84 log10 CFU/mL, respectively, while the total colony count of the chitosan film and composite film treatments increased by 3.23 and 2.93 log10 CFU/mL, respectively, slowing down the proliferation of bacteria and providing a good prolonged preservation effect.

During storage, pork from different treatments were weighed separately at regular intervals and analyzed for moisture loss and quality preservation. It can be seen from Fig. 8E that the quality of pork in the group without film packaging decreased faster, which may be attributed to the loss of moisture and the degradation of microorganisms. The quality of pork in the normal cling film group decreased faster than that of the CS film group after three days of storage. At 10 d of storage, the quality of pork packed with CS film group (88.05 % ± 0.24) and CDs-CS2 film group (89.45 % ± 1.20) showed a slightly slower decrease than that of normal cling film (87.02 % ± 0.76), indicating the advantages of their long shelf life and good water resistance. These minor quality losses are also seen in previous studies of preservation of meat and fruit, which are within the acceptable range to meet consumer expectations for quality preservation of packaged foods (Lan et al., 2020; Yuan et al., 2022; Zhou et al., 2021).

The migration of the composite films in the food simulant was measured using 3 % acetic acid. The UV absorption spectra of the composite film co-infiltrated with the simulated food solution are shown in Fig. 8F. The peak around 290 nm indicated the presence of turmeric carbon quantum dots that released from the composite film. The absorption peak tended to increase with time. However, the change was not significant and the absorbance kept at a low level (below 0.2), indicating that the negligible migration levels of CDs composite film. Even in exceptional cases of migration into packaged food products, the migration was very trace and well below the limit of toxic concentration. This may be due to the fact that carbon quantum dots are more stable in the film matrix and can meet the safety requirements of most food packaging materials on the market.

During storage, both microorganisms and metabolism act together to cause physical and chemical changes in the sample. Therefore, sensory evaluation is also a routine method for testing the freshness of pork. We conducted a comprehensive rating including color, odor, texture, and overall acceptability. As shown in Fig. 9 , the color, odor, and overall acceptability scores of pork gradually decreased as the storage time increased. At day 5 of refrigerated storage, the gap between groups gradually became larger, with the pork with CDs-CD2 films getting the highest score while the control group scoring in the unacceptable range (close to 2). Until the seventh day, the pork wrapped in CDs-CD2 film had acceptable sensory quality with higher scores than the other groups, and the spoilage rate slowed down. The control group had mucus adhering to the surface of the meat on the tenth day, with spoilage odors developing, darkening in color, and hardening in texture. Therefore, the score of control group was the lowest and very unacceptable overall. However, the overall rating of CS film and CDs-CS2 film were still higher than 2. This can be explained by changes in the pH of the meat during storage, which affect the water-holding capacity and texture of the meat, and by changes in the color of the meat due to oxidation of lipids and myoglobin, etc. (Eljoudi et al., 2022; Hernández-García et al., 2022). The results showed that the composite films were the most effective in preserving pork, which can effectively maintain the sensory quality and extend shelf life of pork.

In this study, a series of CDs-CS composite films were prepared by adding different ratios of turmeric carbon quantum dots with photodynamic antibacterial properties on the base material of CS. The prepared composite films were smooth and uniform, with good mechanical properties, UV shielding properties and other physicochemical indexes, which met the requirements of good packaging materials. CDs-CS composite films combined with 405 nm irradiation can produce a large amount of reactive oxygen species, which had a stronger effect of inhibiting S. aureus and E. coli. CDs-CS2 showed the strongest antimicrobial ability at 40 min of light exposure time. Applying it to pork storage and preservation, it can effectively inhibit the growth of microorganisms, slow down its spoilage rate and maintain its good appearance and quality. This study lays a solid foundation for the development and application of chitosan films as new active antibacterial films in food packaging.

Fangzhou Wen: Investigation, Writing-Original Draft, Formal analysis, Data visualization, Data analysi; Peiyuan Li: Conceptualization, Methodology, Resources, Formal analysis, Project administration, Supervision, Writing review & editing; Hongjun Yan: Methodology, Data visualization; Wei Su: Methodology, Formal analysis, Data visualization, Writing review & editing.
